Control: POLICY ON THE SECURITY OF NETWORK AND INFORMATION SYSTEMS
Control Description: A policy on the security of network and information systems shall set out the entity's approach to managing security; be appropriate to and complementary with business strategy and objectives; set out security objectives; include commitment to continual improvement and provision of necessary resources including staff, financial resources, processes, tools and technologies; be communicated to and acknowledged by relevant employees and external parties; lay down roles and responsibilities; list documentation to be retained and retention duration; list topic-specific policies; lay down indicators and measures to monitor implementation and maturity level; and indicate the date of formal approval by management bodies.
Domain: POLICY ON THE SECURITY OF NETWORK AND INFORMATION SYSTEMS
Implementation Guidance: Establish a policy covering all systems, assets and procedures in scope and ensure relevant personnel and external parties acknowledge it through signed or digital acknowledgement, which may be included in employment or service contracts. Communicate the policy in relevant, accessible and understandable form; personnel may receive only relevant extracts or summaries according to their role without disclosure of confidential information. Ensure personnel are aware of their security responsibilities and that both the main policy and topic-specific policies are approved by appropriate management levels. Include detailed guidance on procedures for managing policy exceptions and take care not to disclose confidential information when distributing outside the entity.

Control: POLICY ON THE SECURITY OF NETWORK AND INFORMATION SYSTEMS
Control Description: The network and information system security policy shall be reviewed and, where appropriate, updated by management bodies at least annually and when significant incidents or significant changes to operations or risks occur. The result of the reviews shall be documented.
Domain: POLICY ON THE SECURITY OF NETWORK AND INFORMATION SYSTEMS
Implementation Guidance: Review the policy on the security of network and information systems at least annually considering updates to risk assessment and risk treatment plan, changes in legislation, recommendations from authorities, changes in industry practices, feedback from interested parties, findings from compliance monitoring and independent reviews including policy violations or exceptions, and incidents affecting the entity or similar entities. Update the policy and topic-specific policies based on new findings affecting the entity's security approach, including updates to risk assessment and risk treatment plan, changes to network and information systems, changes to the operating environment, problems identified during implementation, findings from compliance monitoring and independent reviews, status of preventive and corrective actions, threat and vulnerability trends, and known security incidents. All updates shall be based on the entity's unique security risks identified through risk assessment. Obtain management body approval for revised policies and policy exceptions.

Control Description: As part of their policy on the security of network and information systems referred to in point 1.1, the relevant entities shall lay down responsibilities and authorities for network and information system security and assign them t. roles, allocate them according to the relevant entities’ needs and communicate them to the management bodies.
Domain: POLICY ON THE SECURITY OF NETWORK AND INFORMATION SYSTEMS
Implementation Guidance: Write job descriptions in a way that clearly outlines rights and responsibilities. Assign security roles and responsibilities to personnel and include these roles in the organisational chart. Describe roles and assign corresponding responsibilities (e.g. chief information security officer), based on the guidance of international frameworks and standards, including the European cybersecurity skills framework (ECSF) (11). Ensure that the roles allocated are suitable for the size and business needs of the entity. Formally appoint competent personnel in security roles. Ensure that these assigned persons are competent on the basis of appropriate education, training or experience.

Control Description: The relevant entities shall require all personnel and third parties to apply network and information system security in accordance with the established network and information security policy, topic-specific policies and procedures of the relevant entities.
Domain: POLICY ON THE SECURITY OF NETWORK AND INFORMATION SYSTEMS
Implementation Guidance: Make personnel aware of security roles in the entity and when to contact them, and of their security obligations according to their role. Make relevant external parties aware of their security obligations, considering third parties not directly involved in operations but that may affect security. Communicate security requirements in relevant, accessible and understandable form; the full policy, topic-specific policies and procedures do not necessarily need to be communicated in their entirety.

Control Description: At least one person shall report directly to the management bodies on matters of network and information system security.
Domain: POLICY ON THE SECURITY OF NETWORK AND INFORMATION SYSTEMS
Implementation Guidance: Appoint a person (e.g. chief information security officer or information security manager) responsible for overseeing network and information security matters. Make sure that this role is recognized and duly authorized by management bodies.

Control Description: Depending on the size of the relevant entities, network and information system security shall be covered by dedicated roles or duties carried out in addition to existing roles.
Domain: POLICY ON THE SECURITY OF NETWORK AND INFORMATION SYSTEMS
Implementation Guidance: It is often practical to have dedicated information security roles (e.g. a chief information security officer or security analysts) who focus solely on protecting the entity’s data and systems. In entities with limited resources, information security responsibilities may be distributed among existing roles. For instance, information technology (IT) staff might take on security duties alongside their regular tasks. However, the persons assigned should have relevant experience and training to perform the roles and to exercise their responsibilities.

Control Description: Conflicting duties and conflicting areas of responsibility shall be segregated, where applicable.
Domain: POLICY ON THE SECURITY OF NETWORK AND INFORMATION SYSTEMS
Implementation Guidance: Consider segregating conflicting duties and areas of responsibility to reduce opportunities for unauthorized or unintentional modification or misuse of the entity’s asset. As a minimum, consider that the reviewer (auditor) must be different from the personnel or the line of authority of the area under review. The results of the risk assessment or the business impact analysis (BIA) could be used to identify potential conflicting duties and areas of responsibility.

Control Description: Roles, responsibilities and authorities shall be reviewed and, where appropriate, updated by management bodies at planned intervals and when significant incidents or significant changes to operations or risks occur.
Domain: POLICY ON THE SECURITY OF NETWORK AND INFORMATION SYSTEMS
Implementation Guidance: Regularly review and revise the structure of security roles and responsibilities, based on (indicative, non- exhaustive list):. significant incidents, if any;. changes to the environment of operation, including changes to the network and information systems;. organisational changes. Where appropriate, updates should be approved by management bodies.

Control Description: For the purpose of Article 21(2), point (a) of Directive (EU) 2022/2555, the relevant entities shall establish and maintain an appropriate risk management framework to identify and address the risks posed to the security of network and information systems. The relevant entities shall perform and document risk assessments and, based on the results, establish, implement and monitor a risk treatment plan. Risk assessment results and residual risks (12) shall be accepted by management bodies or, where applicable, by persons who are accountable and have the authority to manage risks, provided that the relevant entities ensure adequate reporting to the management bodies.
Implementation Guidance: Use a current or new risk management framework as a structured approach to identify, assess, manage and mitigate cybersecurity risks. Create a risk treatment plan that associates identified risks with assets and mitigating measures, including a description of each identified risk and its impact on security objectives, the chosen risk treatment option, associated assets, mitigating measures, procedures for assessing measure effectiveness, implementation timelines, and responsible roles. Consider residual risks from third parties including data breaches, unaddressed vulnerabilities, regulatory non-compliance and over-reliance on single third parties, and ensure residual risks are accepted by management bodies or appropriate accountable persons in line with acceptable residual risk levels. Obtain management body approval of risk assessment results and risk treatment plan.

Control Description: Relevant entities shall establish procedures for identification, analysis, assessment and treatment of risks that are integral to the overall risk management process. Entities shall follow a risk management methodology, establish risk tolerance in accordance with risk appetite, establish and maintain relevant risk criteria, identify and document risks posed to network and information systems including those related to third parties and risks to availability, integrity, authenticity and confidentiality including single points of failure, analyse risks including threat, likelihood, impact and risk level with consideration of cyber threat intelligence and vulnerabilities, evaluate identified risks based on risk criteria, identify and prioritise appropriate risk treatment options and measures, continuously monitor implementation of risk treatment measures, identify responsibility and timelines for implementing measures, and document chosen risk treatment measures in a risk treatment plan with documented justification for acceptance of residual risks.
Implementation Guidance: Select a risk management methodology and establish the entity's risk appetite as the amount of risk strategically willing to be accepted to achieve objectives, considering business strategic objectives, stakeholder expectations, regulatory requirements and organizational culture. Define risk tolerance level referring to risk willing to be accepted in pursuit of long-term objectives, such as acceptable downtime for critical systems, tolerance for data loss, maximum financial loss, investment percentage in measures, adherence to regulatory obligations, acceptable customer dissatisfaction levels, acceptance of certain vulnerabilities with mitigation measures, incident response timeframes, and acceptance of minor incidents as normal operations. Define risk acceptance criteria including accepting low severity risks, low likelihood risks, risks where mitigation cost exceeds potential impact, specific compliance risks with remediation plans, risks in low-criticality systems, vulnerabilities for defined periods pending remediation, risks with impact below financial thresholds, risks after stakeholder agreement, and residual risks where existing measures reduce likelihood or impact to acceptable levels. Define risk criteria for evaluating risk significance including alignment with risk appetite, regulatory and legal exposure prioritizing GDPR violations and compliance risks, reputational impact from media-attracting incidents, risk velocity from zero-day exploits, recovery complexity from legacy system attacks, emerging technology risks, and stakeholder sensitivity. Identify main risks to network and information systems considering main threats to assets in scope, associate each risk with risk treatment options and specific measures in line with risk assessment and policy, develop risk treatment plans addressing implementation details, and assign responsibilities to appropriate individuals or teams.

Control Description: When identifying and prioritising appropriate risk treatment options and measures, the relevant entities shall take into account the risk assessment results, the results of the procedure to assess the effectiveness of cybersecurity risk- management measures, the cost of implementation in relation to the expected benefit, the asset classification referred to in point 12.1 and the business impact analysis referred to in point

Control: BUSINESS CONTINUITY AND DISASTER RECOVERY PLAN
Control Description: GUIDANCE. Make sure that personnel take into account the elements referred to in point 2.1.3 of the Annex to the regulation. EXAMPLES OF EVIDENCE. Guidance for personnel on assessing risks that takes into account the elements referred to in point 2.1.3 of the Annex to the regulation.

The relevant entities shall review and, where appropriate, update the risk assessment results and the risk treatment plan at planned intervals and at least annually and when significant changes to operations or risks or significant incidents occur.
Implementation Guidance: Review risk assessment results and risk treatment at least annually taking into account:. results of audits and previous reviews,. status of implementation of the measures described in the risk treatment plan (see the policy on the assessment of the effectiveness of measures in line with the Annex to Regulation, point 7.1.1);. changes to the information systems;. changes to the environment of operation;. post-incident review findings (section 3.6); and. trends and changes related to threats and vulnerabilities, as they may affect risks.

Control Description: The relevant entities shall regularly review the compliance with their policies on network and information system security, topic-specific policies, rules and standards. The management bodies shall be informed of the status of network and information security on the basis of the compliance reviews by means of regular reporting.
Implementation Guidance: Develop a standardized report format for reporting to management bodies. Consider the following elements (indicative, non-exhaustive list):. key metrics,. compliance status, including policy exceptions,. identified risks and. recommended actions. Reports are generated and presented to management bodies at least annually.

Control Description: The relevant entities shall put in place an effective compliance reporting system which shall be appropriate t. their structures, operating environments and threat landscapes. The compliance reporting system shall be capable t. provide to the management bodies an informed view of the current state of the relevant entities’ management of risks.
Implementation Guidance: Set up procedures for compliance monitoring, including (indicative, non-exhaustive list):. objectives and a high-level approach to compliance monitoring;. relevant security policies that are subject to compliance monitoring;. the frequency of compliance reviews;. who should carry out compliance reviews (internal or external); and. templates for compliance review reports. Analyse and evaluate the results of the compliance review.

Control Description: The relevant entities shall perform the compliance monitoring at planned intervals and when significant incidents or significant changes to operations or risks occur.
Implementation Guidance: Compliance monitoring should take place at least annually, taking into account:. significant incidents, if any;. changes to the environment of operation;. changes to the threat landscape and cybersecurity legal and regulatory requirements;. changes to standards; and. changes to the policy on the security of network and information systems and/or topic-specific policies. TECHNICAL IMPLEMENTATION GUIDANCE June 2025, version 1.0 28

Control: CYBERSECURITY RISK MANAGEMENT MEASURES EFFECTIVENESS ASSESSMENT
Control Description: Evidence that reports of lessons learned or root cause analysis of past incidents, if any, were taken int. account. 2.3 INDEPENDENT REVIEW OF INFORMATION AND NETWORK SECURITY
Domain: CYBERSECURITY RISK MANAGEMENT MEASURES EFFECTIVENESS ASSESSMENT

Control: INDEPENDENT REVIEW OF INFORMATION AND NETWORK SECURITY
Control Description: The relevant entities shall review independently their approach to managing network and information system security and its implementation including people, processes and technologies.
Implementation Guidance: Make sure that the independent review is conducted by a person or persons with the appropriate competences (indicative, non-exhaustive list):. cybersecurity technical knowledge, for example cybersecurity frameworks (ISO/IEC 27001, National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) cybersecurity framework, etc.),. industry knowledge,. risk assessment skills,. compliance and regulatory knowledge, for example the NIS2 Directive, the GDPR and the Digital Operational Resilience Act,. good understanding of good practices in auditing and. good understanding of when technical certification and conformance are required and must be documented in the conformance and compliance report, to ensure that global standards are implemented correctly.

Control: INDEPENDENT REVIEW OF INFORMATION AND NETWORK SECURITY
Control Description: The relevant entities shall develop and maintain processes to conduct independent reviews which shall be carried out by individuals with appropriate audit competence. Where the independent review is conducted by staff members of the relevant entity, the persons conducting the reviews shall not be in the line of authority of the personnel of the area under review. If the size of the relevant entities does not allow such separation of line of authority, the relevant entities shall put in place alternative measures to guarantee the impartiality of the reviews.
Implementation Guidance: Establish a process for independent review of information and network security that defines scope and purpose (compliance, risk assessment, policy adherence), methodology (standardised checklist, standard-based, ad hoc, real-time or periodic testing), review committee role, review frequency, whether reviews are internal or external, and report templates. Maintain independence per regulation point 2.3.2, considering alternative measures such as reviewer rotation, cross-departmental review committees, or external third-party providers where separation of line of authority is not feasible.

Control: INDEPENDENT REVIEW OF INFORMATION AND NETWORK SECURITY
Control Description: The results of the independent reviews, including the results from the compliance monitoring pursuant to point 2.2. and the monitoring and measurement pursuant to point 7, shall be reported to the management bodies. Corrective actions shall be taken or residual risk accepted according to the relevant entities’ risk acceptance criteria.
Implementation Guidance: Analyse and evaluate independent review results and report to management bodies at least annually using a standardised format including executive summary with scope and key findings, methodology, detailed findings with gaps and non-compliance issues, recommendations, and conclusions. Take corrective actions or document and justify accepted residual risks. Systematically reflect review outcomes in risk assessments and risk treatment plans, updating the risk assessment whenever risks are identified or reassessed through independent reviews to ensure the risk profile remains accurate and emerging risks are adequately addressed.

Control: CYBERSECURITY RISK MANAGEMENT MEASURES EFFECTIVENESS ASSESSMENT
Control Description: Documentation of any corrective actions. Budget approval for any corrective actions. The most recent results of compliance monitoring and auditing. Records from updated risk register.
Domain: CYBERSECURITY RISK MANAGEMENT MEASURES EFFECTIVENESS ASSESSMENT

Control: INDEPENDENT REVIEW OF INFORMATION AND NETWORK SECURITY
Control Description: The independent reviews shall take place at planned intervals and when significant incidents or significant changes to operations or risks occur.
Implementation Guidance: Independent reviews should take place at least annually, taking into account:. significant incidents, if any;. changes to the environment of operation;. changes to the threat landscape and cybersecurity legal and regulatory requirements; and. changes to the policy on the security of network and information systems and/or topic-specific policies.

Control Description: For the purpose of Article 21(2), point (b) of Directive (EU) 2022/2555, the relevant entities shall establish and implement an incident handling policy laying down the roles, responsibilities and procedures for detecting, analysing, containing or responding to, recovering from, documenting and reporting of incidents in a timely manner.
Implementation Guidance: Define clear objectives for the incident handling policy. Ensure the policy complies with relevant laws, regulations and industry standards (17).

Control Description: The policy referred to in point 3.1.1 shall be coherent with the business continuity and disaster recovery plan referred to in point 4.1. The policy shall include: (a) a categorisation system for incidents that is consistent with the event assessment and classification carried out pursuant to point 3.4.1; (b) effective communication plans including for escalation and reporting; (c) assignment of roles to detect and appropriately respond to incidents to competent employees; (d) documents to be used in the course of incident detection and response such as incident response manuals, escalation charts, contact lists and templates.
Implementation Guidance: Align the incident handling policy with business continuity and disaster recovery plans by ensuring both minimise disruptions and enable swift return to normal operations, identifying interfaces between processes, describing workflows that trigger business continuity during incidents, and developing test scenarios documenting the interaction between these processes. Establish an incident categorisation system using criteria such as business impact, data sensitivity, legal and regulatory impact including reporting timelines, scope and scale, attack type, malicious software or vulnerability exploitation, system criticality, containment urgency, data exfiltration or corruption potential, recovery likelihood, impact on human safety, and other significant incident criteria per regulation. Ensure the policy addresses system failures, malicious code, denial of service, errors, breaches of confidentiality and integrity, and misuse of systems. Communicate incidents to relevant stakeholders and personnel using a communication plan that specifies purpose, roles and responsibilities, internal and external stakeholders to inform, regulatory reporting timelines, escalation procedures, communication channels tailored to audience (internal, client, public), stakeholder feedback methods, and pre-drafted message templates with core messages and communication guidelines. Identify necessary roles and responsibilities for incident handling, utilising established skills frameworks such as the ECSF.

Control Description: The roles, responsibilities and procedures laid down in the policy shall be tested and reviewed and, where appropriate, updated at planned intervals and after significant incidents or significant changes to operations or risks.
Implementation Guidance: Test the incident handling policy at least annually using tabletop exercises, incident simulations based on selected attack scenarios aligned with identified risks and threat landscape, red team/blue team exercises, or past incident walk-throughs to validate roles, responsibilities, and procedures. Review and update roles, responsibilities, and procedures at least annually based on policy test results, changes to the threat landscape and cybersecurity legal or regulatory requirements, and changes to network and information systems security policies or topic-specific policies.

Control Description: The relevant entities shall lay down procedures and use tools to monitor and log activities on their network and information systems to detect events that could be considered as incidents and respond accordingly to mitigate the impact.
Implementation Guidance: Identify monitoring and logging objectives including threat detection, compliance assurance, incident response support, performance optimisation, anomaly detection, vulnerability monitoring for free and open source software components, data loss prevention, forensic investigation support, and network health monitoring. Monitoring procedures must describe objectives, data for collection and relevant tools, data algorithms, and notification mechanisms for relevant personnel. Select tools based on ease of use, integration with existing network and information systems including cross-border operations and associated regulatory and security considerations, minimisation of manual intervention, data collection capability from various sources (networks, systems, applications), security features (encryption, access control), and costs and licencing.

Control Description: To the extent feasible, monitoring shall be automated and carried out either continuously or in periodic intervals, subject to business capabilities. The relevant entities shall implement their monitoring activities in a way which minimises false positives and false negatives.
Implementation Guidance: To minimise false positives and false negatives, to the extent feasible, consider one or more of the following (indicative, non-exhaustive list):. establish network traffic patterns;. use analytics and machine learning algorithms;. continuously update the automated monitoring tools to adapt to new threats and changes in the environment; and. fine-tune the parameters and thresholds based on the latest data and feedback. Where appropriate, ensure that all potential risks are properly covered by relevant use cases, for example use case for access to critical data, use case for data exfiltration or use case for ransomware infection, s. that no critical threat goes undetected.

Control Description: Relevant entities shall maintain, document and review logs established based on risk assessment results. Logs shall include: relevant outbound and inbound network traffic; creation, modification or deletion of users and permission extensions; access to systems and applications; authentication-related events; all privileged access and administrative account activities; access or changes to critical configuration and backup files; event logs and security tool logs such as antivirus, intrusion detection systems or firewalls; use and performance of system resources; physical access to facilities; access to and use of network equipment and devices; activation, stopping and pausing of logs; and environmental events.
Implementation Guidance: With regard to critical configuration, consider the settings and parameters that are vital for the proper functioning, security and performance of the entity’s network and information system. These configurations are vital because any changes or misconfigurations might have a significant impact, including system outages, security vulnerabilities or reduced performance of the entity’s network and information system. Consult the risk assessment results to determine which network traffic needs to be logged. For example, if certain assets are identified as high risk (e.g. due to being potentially vulnerable or crucial to the business operation) their inbound and outbound traffic should be logged for monitoring and analysis.

Control Description: The logs shall be regularly reviewed for any unusual or unwanted trends. Where appropriate, the relevant entities shall lay down appropriate values for alarm thresholds. If the laid down values for alarm threshold are exceeded, an alarm shall be triggered, where appropriate, automatically. The relevant entities shall ensure that, in case of an alarm, a qualified and appropriate response is initiated in a timely manner.
Implementation Guidance: Design procedures using anomaly detection or adaptive alarm thresholds to detect network-based attacks from anomalous traffic patterns and denial of service attacks timely. Set alarm thresholds aligned with risk assessment results covering at least the logging situations in point 3.2.3, including: traffic volume spikes exceeding 50% of normal traffic in 10 minutes on specific ports; three or more account lockouts within 15 minutes; two or more privilege escalations within 24 hours; malware detected on multiple endpoints within short timeframes; and unauthorized software installations within short timeframes.

Control Description: The relevant entities shall maintain and back up logs for a predefined period and shall protect them from unauthorised access or changes.
Implementation Guidance: Make sure that the log retention period is defined in accordance with business needs, the risk assessment results and legal requirements/obligations. The backup logs’ maintenance period shouldn’t be shorter than the logs’ review period, referred to in point 3.2.4 of the Annex to the regulation. The retention period should be in line with what is referred to in point 4.2.2 (f) of the Annex to the regulation. Delete data when the retention period ends. Consider mechanisms to protect logs from unauthorised access or changes (indicative, non-exhaustive list):. encryption,. access control,. hashing (section 9.2) and. logging of all access and changes to log files. The access control should be in line with what is referred to in point 4.2.2 (d) of the Annex to the regulation.

Control Description: To the extent feasible, the relevant entities shall ensure that all systems have synchronised time sources to be able to correlate logs between systems for event assessment. The relevant entities shall establish and keep a list of all assets that are being logged and ensure that monitoring and logging systems are redundant. The availability of the monitoring and logging systems shall be monitored independent of the systems they are monitoring.
Implementation Guidance: Use authenticated Network Time Protocol (NTP) servers or Precision Time Protocol (PTP) for time synchronization, configure a central time server within the entity synchronized with reliable external sources, and use multiple time sources to avoid single points of failure. Plan time synchronization across on-premises systems, cloud services, and SaaS platforms, especially in hybrid environments. Mark assets being logged in the asset inventory per point 12.4. Implement measures to protect log data against loss including redundant storage across multiple locations, retention of processed log events in structured systems, and preservation of derived security insights per point 4.2. Deploy separate tools to monitor capacity and availability of primary monitoring and logging systems.

Control Description: The procedures as well as the list of assets that are being logged shall be reviewed and, where appropriate, updated at regular intervals and after significant incidents.
Implementation Guidance: Determine the frequency of reviews based on the risk assessment results related to the criticality of the assets, ensuring that reviews are conducted at least annually. Include the testing of monitoring and logging procedures in security testing (section 6.5). Review a random sample of logs to verify that all the assets that should be subject to the log are actually considered.

Control Description: The relevant entities shall put in place a simple mechanism allowing their employees, suppliers and customers t. report suspicious events.
Implementation Guidance: Define suspicious events based on whether confidentiality, integrity or availability of the network or information system is affected, persistence of the event, impact such as number of affected assets, and compliance violations. Develop guidelines for report information aligned with what may be submitted to CSIRT or competent authority under NIS2 Directive Articles 23 or 30, including minimum reporting of date and time, event description, relevant screenshots and logs or evidence, and follow-up contact information. Provide multiple reporting channels such as email, web form, dedicated phone line or mobile app that are easily accessible and intuitive.

Control Description: The relevant entities shall, where appropriate, communicate the event reporting mechanism to their suppliers and customers and shall regularly train their employees how to use the mechanism.
Implementation Guidance: Make appropriate means for reporting available to personnel and the entity’s suppliers and customers. Consider anonymous reporting to encourage individuals to report security events without fear of reprisal. Take into account legal obligations to report an incident to the competent authorities (and CSIRTs) in line with the NIS2 Directive Articles 23 and 30, including any obligations about when the incident should be reported. Regularly remind stakeholders of the reporting mechanism through email newsletters, posters and other communication channels. Conduct regular exercises or simulations to test the effectiveness of the reporting mechanism.

Control Description: The relevant entities shall assess suspicious events to determine whether they constitute incidents and, if so, determine their nature and severity.
Implementation Guidance: Use criteria to assess whether a suspicious event is an incident or not. The guidance in section 3.1.2 provides an indicative, non-exhaustive list of such criteria. Determine the nature and severity of the event based on a categorisation system referred to in point 3.1.2 (a) of the Annex to the regulation.

Control Description: For the purpose of point 3.4.1, the relevant entities shall act in the following manner: (a) carry out the assessment based on predefined criteria laid down in advance and on a triage to determine prioritisation of incident containment and eradication; (b) assess the existence of recurring incidents as referred to in Article 4 of this Regulation on a quarterly basis; (c) review the appropriate logs for the purposes of event assessment and classification; (d) put in place a process for log correlation and analysis and (e) reassess and reclassify events in case of new information becoming available or after analysis of previously available information.
Implementation Guidance: Include activities in incident handling procedures for assessing suspicious events to determine nature and severity through gathering relevant information and evidence, analyzing potential impact on systems and operations, and determining severity based on predefined criteria. Implement playbooks or runbooks to guide initial assessment for common incident types such as ransomware, phishing, data or device loss, or fire. Classify events by severity (low, medium, high, critical), type (malware infection, unauthorized access), and regulatory implications. Prioritize events according to the categorization system in the incident handling policy. Perform root cause analysis to determine recurring incidents, noting that this assessment may be delayed in early incident handling stages. Review and correlate logs per point 3.2 and assess past events and their classification to improve processes, procedures and thresholds.

Control Description: The relevant entities shall respond to incidents in accordance with documented procedures and in a timely manner.
Implementation Guidance: Establish a dedicated incident response team comprising employees with the necessary technical expertise and authority to respond effectively to incidents, where appropriate. Define roles and responsibilities within the incident response team, such as incident coordinators, analysts and communication liaisons, where appropriate. Take into account industry-recognised standards when developing the incident response procedures (33). Implement playbooks or runbooks to guide incident response actions for common types of incidents.

Control Description: The incident response procedures shall include the following stages: (a) incident containment, to prevent the consequences of the incident from spreading; (b) eradication, to prevent the incident from continuing or reappearing, (c) recovery from the incident, where necessary.
Implementation Guidance: Create incident response procedures addressing cybersecurity incident handling that account for entity priorities and incident impact. Recognize and resolve conflicts between forensic activities (preserving evidence for legal/compliance/investigative purposes), incident response activities (mitigating threats and preventing further damage), and operational continuity (minimizing IT service disruption). Establish a clear decision-making process that prioritizes based on accepted risk tolerance, business impact, and legal obligations, involves coordination between cybersecurity, legal/compliance and operational teams, and documents prioritization rationale for transparency and accountability. Develop incident response playbooks incorporating decision-making and escalation paths for managing trade-offs between evidence preservation, threat containment and operational continuity. Keep management bodies informed.

Control Description: The relevant entities shall establish communication plans and procedures: (a) with the Computer Security Incident Response Teams (CSIRTs) or, where applicable, the competent authorities, related to incident notification; (b) with relevant internal and external stakeholders.
Implementation Guidance: Ensure that the communication plan (Annex to the regulation, point 3.1.2) includes procedures for how t. communicate the incident to the relevant authorities, the national CSIRT and internal and external stakeholders, including, where applicable, customers, direct suppliers, service providers and, if open source is used, contacts for free and open source software projects. Include contact information for key personnel, external stakeholders and relevant authorities.

Control Description: The relevant entities shall log incident response activities in accordance with the procedures referred to in point
and record evidence.
Implementation Guidance: Log incident response information which contains (indicative, non-exhaustive list):. time of detection, containment and eradication;. when the systems recovered;. indicators of compromise;. root cause;. actions taken during each phase namely, detection, containment and eradication;. assessment of the scope and the level of impact of the incident;. communications when responding to the incident;. post incident lessons learnt and recommendations; and. whether the CSIRT or the competent authority was notified of the incident according to the NIS2 Directive Articles 23 and 30.

Control Description: The relevant entities shall test at planned intervals their incident response procedures.
Implementation Guidance: Test the entity’s incident response procedures at least annually. Test different types of incidents, for example ransomware, phishing, data breach and DoS. Ensure that test scenarios involve employees from different departments as well as external stakeholders, for example suppliers and service providers. Where necessary, include management bodies in the tests so that they understand their role during an incident. Conduct post-test reviews for possible lessons learnt. Update the incident response procedures based on the lessons learnt from the test, if applicable.

Control Description: Where appropriate, the relevant entities shall carry out post-incident reviews after recovery from incidents. The post-incident reviews shall identify, where possible, the root cause of the incident and result in documented lessons learned to reduce the occurrence and consequences of future incidents.
Implementation Guidance: Conduct root cause analysis (34) and identify the root cause of the incident, where possible. Identify contributing factors and areas for improvement in incident detection, response and recovery processes. Investigate significant incidents and write final incident reports, including actions taken and recommendations to mitigate future occurrence of this type of incident. Document lessons learnt, accompanied by recommendations and their owners, based on logs from incident response referred to in point 3.5.4 of the Annex to the regulation. Share any relevant findings in the post-incident review with affected stakeholders, for example suppliers, service providers, free and open source component maintainers.

Control Description: The relevant entities shall ensure that post-incident reviews contribute to improving their approach to network and information security, to risk treatment measures and to incident handling, detection and response procedures.
Implementation Guidance: Analyse the post-incident review findings to identify gaps and weaknesses in the entity’s network and information security status. Make sure that the identified gaps and weaknesses feed back to the risk assessment and risk treatment plan (Annex to the regulation, point 2.1). Assess whether existing risk treatment measures were effective in preventing or mitigating the incident. Document the findings and lessons learnt from each post-incident review comprehensively. Consider whether information security requirements have been met throughout the handling of a cybersecurity incident or whether measures may need to be taken to restore them (e.g. resetting passwords for emergency administrative access).

Control Description: The relevant entities shall review at planned intervals if incidents led to post-incident reviews.
Implementation Guidance: Conduct an annual review or a review after significant incidents, to determine if an incident has led to a post-incident review.

Control: BUSINESS CONTINUITY AND DISASTER RECOVERY PLAN
Control Description: For the purpose of Article 21(2), point (c) of Directive (EU) 2022/2555, the relevant entities shall lay down and maintain a business continuity and disaster recovery plan to apply in the case of incidents.
Implementation Guidance: Take into account industry-recognised standards when developing the business continuity ( 35) and disaster recovery plan. Create a list of natural disasters (e.g. hurricane, fire, flooding) and other occurrences (e.g. human error) that could affect the services together with a list of disaster recovery capabilities (e.g. backups, tests, recovery objectives, etc.).

Control: BUSINESS CONTINUITY AND DISASTER RECOVERY PLAN
Control Description: The relevant entities’ operations shall be restored according to the business continuity and disaster recovery plan. The plan shall be based on the results of the risk assessment carried out pursuant to point 2.1 and shall include, where appropriate, the following: (a) purpose, scope and audience; (b) roles and responsibilities; (c) key contacts and (internal and external) communication channels; (d) conditions for plan activation and deactivation; (e) order of recovery for operations; (f) recovery plans for specific operations, including recovery objectives; (g) required resources, including backups and redundancies; (h) restoring and resuming activities from temporary measures.
Implementation Guidance: Keep logs of business continuity plan activation and execution including decisions taken, steps followed, and final recovery time. Determine recovery order based on asset classification level, service importance, dependencies, recovery objectives, resource availability, and regulatory requirements. Conduct capacity planning to ensure necessary capacity for information processing, telecommunications and environmental support exists after plan activation. Consider primary and alternate telecommunications providers for disaster recovery. For remote work, employees handling critical operations must have access to backup internet solutions (mobile broadband, tethering) and participate in regular failover testing including VPN and voice communications over backup networks. Prepare for post-disaster service recovery by identifying failover sites in other regions, backing up critical data to remote locations, and testing restore procedures regularly. Ensure third-party services (e.g., hot sites) are available for disasters where appropriate. Implement advanced measures such as full redundancy, failover mechanisms, and alternative sites as appropriate.

Control: BUSINESS CONTINUITY AND DISASTER RECOVERY PLAN
Control Description: The relevant entities shall carry out a business impact analysis to assess the potential impact of severe disruptions to their business operations and shall, based on the results of the business impact analysis, establish continuity requirements for the network and information systems.
Implementation Guidance: Based on business impact analysis and risk assessment results, establish appropriate recovery objectives: Recovery Time Objectives (RTOs) defining maximum recovery time for business resources and functions after disaster; Recovery Point Objectives (RPOs) defining acceptable data loss (measured in maximum recovery time without unacceptable damage); Service Delivery Objectives (SDOs) defining minimum performance levels during alternate processing (e.g., call center response times, e-commerce system availability, remote work infrastructure restoration timeframes); and Maximum Acceptable Outage (MAO) or Maximum Tolerable Period of Disruption (MTPD) determining when service unavailability becomes unacceptable (typically longer than RTOs, focusing on service availability while RPOs focus on data loss). RTOs, RPOs and SDOs may determine backup and redundancy procedures. Document the disaster recovery plan accounting for RTOs, RPOs, SDOs and compliance with applicable regulations.

Control: BUSINESS CONTINUITY AND DISASTER RECOVERY PLAN
Control Description: The business continuity plan and disaster recovery plan shall be tested, reviewed and, where appropriate, updated at planned intervals and following significant incidents or significant changes to operations or risks. The relevant entities shall ensure that the plans incorporate lessons learnt from such tests.
Implementation Guidance: Test, review and update business continuity and disaster recovery plans at least annually using methods such as alternative personnel locations, disaster recovery hot sites, digital twins, simulations, or tabletop exercises. Test regularly considering change logs, past incidents, and previous test results. Test disaster recovery plans at alternate processing sites to familiarize personnel and evaluate site capabilities. Test data centre infrastructure for availability, auto failover, power failover between providers or to backup power, and resilience to maintain customer service. Define full recovery and system reconstitution to a known state as part of testing. Update plans based on change logs, past incidents, test results and training records. Review previous test results and change logs to ensure plans incorporate lessons learned. Review and update roles and responsibilities. Review dependent third parties' disaster recovery plans for compliance with business continuity requirements. Communicate plan changes to relevant personnel.

Control Description: The relevant entities shall maintain backup copies of data and provide sufficient available resources, including facilities, network and information systems and staff, to ensure an appropriate level of redundancy.
Implementation Guidance: Consider whether to invest in own redundancy or to engage third parties, for example cloud providers, t. provide such redundancy, in alignment with the BIA (Annex to the regulation, point 4.1.3).

Control Description: Based on the results of the risk assessment carried out pursuant to point 2.1 and the business continuity plan, the relevant entities shall lay down backup plans which include the following: (a) recovery times; (b) assurance that backup copies are complete and accurate, including configuration data and data stored in cloud computing service environment; (c) storing backup copies (online or offline) in a safe location or locations, which are not in the same network as the system and are at sufficient distance to escape any damage from a disaster at the main site; (d) appropriate physical and logical access controls to backup copies, in accordance with the asset classification level; (e) restoring data from backup copies; (f) retention periods based on business and regulatory requirements.
Implementation Guidance: Recovery times should not exceed the recovery objectives referred to in 4.1.2 (f) of the Annex to the regulation. Concerning retention periods consider what is referred to in point 3.2.5 of the Annex to the regulation. If an entity engages third parties to ensure an appropriate level of redundancy, it should be clearly decided whether it is the entity's responsibility to compile the backup plans or if the third parties have any involvement in the process.

Control Description: The relevant entities shall perform regular integrity checks on the backup copies.
Implementation Guidance: Check the integrity of the backup copies. An indicative, non-exhaustive list of good practices is the following:. use checksums or hashing algorithms to verify that the data in your backups matches the original data (section 9.2);. implement automated scripts to run these checks regularly, reducing the risk of human error;. schedule regular tests to restore data from backups to ensure they are complete, functional and validated by business users to confirm the accuracy and usability of the restored data;. test various recovery scenarios, including full system restores and individual file recoveries, t. ensure all aspects of your backup system are reliable; and. consider using cloud storage solutions for off-site backups, which often include built-in integrity checks and redundancy.

Control Description: Based on the results of the risk assessment carried out pursuant to point 2.1 and the business continuity plan, the relevant entities shall ensure sufficient availability of resources by at least partial redundancy of the following: (a) network and information systems; (b) assets, including facilities, equipment and supplies; (c) personnel with the necessary responsibility, authority and competence; (d) appropriate communication channels.
Implementation Guidance: Define minimum resources needed to ensure at least partial redundancy for: network and information systems (multiple internet service providers, load balancing, mirrored servers, virtualization, redundant disk arrays); assets (shared workspaces, backup locations, spare equipment, multiple suppliers); personnel (job rotation, backup assignments, emergency drills); multiple communication platforms (social media, messaging apps, email); and multiple methods to power a site through multiple electrical providers or combination of providers with backup mechanisms such as generators.

Control Description: Where appropriate, the relevant entities shall ensure that monitoring and adjustment of resources, including facilities, systems and personnel, is duly informed by backup and redundancy requirements.
Implementation Guidance: Decisions about resource allocation and adjustments should be guided by the need for backups and redundancy. To this end, the entity might consider one or more of the following (indicative, non-exhaustive list):. prioritisation of resources based on the results of the risk analysis;. partial redundancy;. diverse backup locations; and. continuous monitoring of the resources where redundancy is necessary. TECHNICAL IMPLEMENTATION GUIDANCE June 2025, version 1.0 59

Control Description: The relevant entities shall carry out regular testing of the recovery of backup copies and redundancies to ensure that, in recovery conditions, they can be relied upon and cover the copies, processes and knowledge to perform an effective recovery. The relevant entities shall document the results of the tests and, where needed, take corrective action.
Implementation Guidance: Tailor the frequency of the backup checks to the data criticality based on the risk assessment (section 2.1). As an example:. Data with high criticality might be checked on a weekly basis. Data with moderate and low criticality might be checked on a monthly basis. Significant changes should be checked immediately after the change. Make sure that the issues and lessons learnt from exercises are addressed by the responsible people and that the relevant processes and systems are updated accordingly. Involve suppliers and other third parties, such as business partners or customers in tests.

Control Description: The relevant entities shall put in place a process for crisis management.
Implementation Guidance: Develop crisis management processes using industry-recognised standards such as ISO 22361:2022, ENISA Best Practices for Cyber Crisis Management, and NIST SP 800-61 Revision 2, considering technical, operational, communication and remediation aspects including roles and responsibilities. Define clear criteria for escalating incidents to crisis status based on the entity's risk appetite and incident handling capabilities, such as incidents posing significant risk to critical assets or operations, causing serious business disruption or prolonged downtime, affecting multiple systems or geographic locations, impacting reputation or data confidentiality, integrity, authenticity and availability, involving advanced persistent threats or organised cybercrime, or having potential to escalate further through vulnerability exploitation or malware spread.

Control Description: The relevant entities shall ensure that the crisis management process addresses at least the following elements: (a) roles and responsibilities for personnel and, where appropriate, suppliers and service providers, specifying the allocation of roles in crisis situations, including specific steps to follow; (b) appropriate communication means between the relevant entities and relevant competent authorities; (c) application of appropriate measures to ensure the maintenance of network and information system security in crisis situations. For the purpose of point (b), the flow of information between the relevant entities and relevant competent authorities shall include both obligatory communications, such as incident reports and related timelines and non-obligatory communications.
Implementation Guidance: Develop crisis communication procedures addressing legal notification obligations and timing requirements, methods for disseminating information to internal and external stakeholders (employees, customers, suppliers, emergency services, competent authorities), communication templates, appropriate channels for each stakeholder type recognising that normal channels may be unsafe during crisis and different stakeholders use different channels, and maintain up-to-date contact information for all internal and external stakeholders.

Control Description: The relevant entities shall implement a process for managing and making use of information received from the CSIRTs or, where applicable, the competent authorities, concerning incidents, vulnerabilities, threats or possible mitigation measures.
Implementation Guidance: Implement a process for managing CSIRT information by designating a point of contact with sufficient incident and threat intelligence knowledge, classifying incoming information into incidents, vulnerabilities, threats and mitigation measures, assigning priority levels based on severity and potential impact, reviewing information for relevance and urgency, validating against internal logs and threat intelligence feeds, collaborating with relevant teams to develop mitigation strategies, updating incident response plans accordingly, implementing mitigation measures and communicating with stakeholders, and voluntarily sharing incident insights and feedback with the CSIRT.

Control Description: The relevant entities shall test, review and, where appropriate, update the crisis management plan on a regular basis or following significant incidents or significant changes to operations or risks.
Implementation Guidance: Test the crisis management process annually through exercises or simulations accounting for past crisis situations, comparing test results against defined objectives such as RTOs, RPOs and SDOs, and using results to update and improve procedures. Review and update the crisis management process, security policy and organisational measures following any test, significant incidents, or significant changes to operations or risks.

Control Description: For the purpose of Article 21(2), point (d) of Directive (EU) 2022/2555, the relevant entities shall establish, implement and apply a supply chain security policy which governs the relations with their direct suppliers and service providers to mitigate the identified risks to the security of network and information systems. In the supply chain security policy, the relevant entities shall identify their role in the supply chain and communicate it to their direct suppliers and service providers.
Implementation Guidance: Develop supply chain policy using industry-recognised standards or good practices. Suppliers or service providers may include ICT suppliers, manufacturers, managed service providers, managed security service providers and cloud computing providers. Free and open source software communities and projects are not considered direct suppliers or service providers where no contractual relationship exists beyond adherence to standardised copyright licences, or where the contractual relationship is with an open source software steward providing sustained development support and ensuring product viability.

Control Description: Relevant entities shall establish criteria to select and contract suppliers and service providers based on: their cybersecurity practices including secure development procedures; their ability to meet specified cybersecurity requirements; the overall quality, resilience and cybersecurity risk-management measures of ICT products and services including risk classification; and their ability to diversify supply sources and limit vendor lock-in where applicable.
Implementation Guidance: Consider the supplier's legal jurisdiction and regulatory status under NIS2 or the Cyber Resilience Act; compliance statements and corporate ownership; ability to ensure supply including size and control over their own supply chain; cybersecurity practices attested by industry certifications, vendor risk-management reports or assessment questionnaires; notices from national authorities; sensitivity of acquired products and services; history of cybersecurity events and breaches; and possibility of vendor lock-in considering use of open standards, open data formats, existing contracts and proprietary features.

Control Description: When establishing their supply chain security policy, relevant entities shall take into account the results of the coordinated security risk assessments of critical supply chains carried out in accordance with Article 22(1) of Directive (EU) 2022/2555, where applicable.
Implementation Guidance: Take into account any recommendations or guidance on supply chain security, published by the NIS Cooperation Group, established by the NIS2 Directive, Article 14 ( 42) and by the national competent authorities. (42) ‘NIS Cooperation Group’, https://digital-strategy.ec.europa.eu/en/policies/nis-cooperation-group. TECHNICAL IMPLEMENTATION GUIDANCE June 2025, version 1.0 68

Control Description: Contracts with suppliers and service providers shall specify through service level agreements where appropriate: cybersecurity requirements including those for ICT service and product acquisition; employee awareness, skills, training and certification requirements; employee background verification requirements; obligation to notify without undue delay of incidents presenting security risk; right to audit or receive audit reports; obligation to handle vulnerabilities presenting security risk; requirements for subcontracting with equivalent cybersecurity standards; and obligations upon contract termination including retrieval and disposal of obtained information.
Implementation Guidance: Include all requirements from point 5.1.4 in new and renewed contracts. For large suppliers and service providers, consider collective bargaining, association representation, legal review, negotiation of exit and SLA clauses, suppliers with public security commitments, those with standard certifications like ISO 27001 or SOC 2, trusted vetted platforms, contract templates from business associations, tools allowing easy data export with no lock-in, avoiding long-term commitments without clear exit strategies, and maintaining a list of alternative suppliers. The entity remains fully accountable for service integrity, availability and confidentiality.

Control Description: The relevant entities shall take into account the elements referred to in point 5.1.2 and
as part of the selection process of new suppliers and service providers, as well as part of the procurement process referred to in point 6.1.
Implementation Guidance: Perform a risk analysis before entering into any agreement with suppliers and service providers related t. information security, taking into account the elements referred to in point 5.1.2 and 5.1.3, where appropriate (44).

Control Description: Comparison between selected contracts and the associated tenders to check whether the secure acquisition of ICT systems, products and service processes and particularly the elements referred to in point 6.1.2 of the Annex to the regulation, are taken into consideration. Risk analysis results from supplier and service provider evaluations.

The relevant entities shall review the supply chain security policy and monitor, evaluate and, where necessary, act upon changes in the cybersecurity practices of suppliers and service providers, at planned intervals and when significant changes to operations or risks or significant incidents related to the provision of ICT services or having impact on the security of the ICT products from suppliers and service providers occur.
Implementation Guidance: Review the supply chain policy at least annually. Create and maintain a process to monitor suppliers and service providers over the life cycle.

Control Description: For the purpose of point 5.1.6., the relevant entities shall: (a) regularly monitor reports on the implementation of the service level agreements, where applicable; (b) review incidents related to ICT products and ICT services from suppliers and service providers; (c) assess the need for unscheduled reviews and document the findings in a comprehensible manner; (d) analyse the risks presented by changes related to ICT products and ICT services from suppliers and service providers and, where appropriate, take mitigating measures in a timely manner.
Implementation Guidance: Conduct regular reviews of supplier compliance with agreed SLAs and follow up on deviations; define and assign responsibilities for asset maintenance, operation and ownership; monitor periodic reassessment of compliance and supplier release notes; ensure product configuration aligns with vendor recommendations with increasing frequency as products age; track security incidents related to suppliers as they may trigger unscheduled reviews; conduct unscheduled reviews upon material changes in supplier operations, changes in risk exposure, failure to meet contractual obligations, or emergence of new threats or vulnerabilities. Monitoring frequency shall align with supply chain policy and secure acquisition review processes.

Control: SECURITY IN ACQUISITION OF ICT SERVICES OR ICT PRODUCTS
Control Description: For the purpose of Article 21(2), point (e) of Directive (EU) 2022/2555, the relevant entities shall set and implement processes to manage risks stemming from the acquisition of ICT services or ICT products for components that are critical for the relevant entities’ security of network and information systems, based on the risk assessment carried out pursuant to point 2.1, from suppliers or service providers throughout their life cycle.
Domain: SECURITY IN NETWORK AND INFORMATION SYSTEMS ACQUISITION, DEVELOPMENT AND MAINTENANCE
Implementation Guidance: Integrate cybersecurity as a permanent component of the purchase process by dedicating a specific section to addressing it. This includes any acquisition processes for selecting FOSS (48). Document the process for secure acquisition of ICT services, systems or products and describe relevant procedures that support the process. Take into account industry-recognised standards when developing the process (49).

Control: SECURITY IN ACQUISITION OF ICT SERVICES OR ICT PRODUCTS
Control Description: For the purpose of point 6.1.1., the processes referred to in point
Domain: SECURITY IN NETWORK AND INFORMATION SYSTEMS ACQUISITION, DEVELOPMENT AND MAINTENANCE

Control: SECURITY IN ACQUISITION OF ICT SERVICES OR ICT PRODUCTS
Control Description: Acquisition processes shall include: security requirements for ICT services or products; requirements for security updates throughout the entire lifetime or replacement after support ends; information describing hardware and software components; information describing implemented cybersecurity functions and configuration required for secure operation; assurance of compliance with security requirements; and methods for validating delivered ICT services or products against security requirements with documented results.
Domain: SECURITY IN NETWORK AND INFORMATION SYSTEMS ACQUISITION, DEVELOPMENT AND MAINTENANCE
Implementation Guidance: Security requirements must include means to detect, monitor and protect against unauthorized software and information changes. Support contracts must cover the system life cycle and obsolescence management with defined support end dates and continuous alerting. Favour vendors providing clear end-of-life information and separate critical security fixes; request suppliers provide tested security issue solutions free of charge as soon as they become known. Describe implemented cybersecurity functions including potential risks, required security tools such as firewalls or SIEM, specific security mechanisms like encryption or MFA, applicable cybersecurity standards, required assurance levels and relevant European Cybersecurity Scheme certificates. Evaluate ICT service or product security before acquisition.

Control: SECURITY IN ACQUISITION OF ICT SERVICES OR ICT PRODUCTS
Control Description: The relevant entities shall review and, where appropriate, update the processes at planned intervals and when significant incidents occur.
Domain: SECURITY IN NETWORK AND INFORMATION SYSTEMS ACQUISITION, DEVELOPMENT AND MAINTENANCE
Implementation Guidance: Review the processes for secure acquisition of ICT services, systems or products, and the procedures based on them, at least annually. Review logs or records of all changes made to the processes for secure acquisition of ICT services, systems or products, and the procedures based on them, including details of the changes, approvals and implementation dates. Align the tenders and contracts with the entity’s supply chain security policy (Annex to the regulation, point 5.1). For ICT services, systems or products that are not provided by a supplier (e.g. open source projects), entities should share relevant results from internal assessments with them.

Control Description: Before developing a network and information system, including software, the relevant entities shall lay down rules for the secure development of network and information systems and apply them when developing network and information systems in-house or when outsourcing the development of network and information systems. The rules shall cover all development phases, including specification, design, development, implementation and testing.
Domain: SECURITY IN NETWORK AND INFORMATION SYSTEMS ACQUISITION, DEVELOPMENT AND MAINTENANCE
Implementation Guidance: Take into account the entity’s policies and norms (if available) and industry-recognised standards when developing the rules for the secure development of network and information systems (51).

Control Description: Relevant entities shall: carry out security requirement analysis at specification and design phases of development or acquisition projects; apply secure systems engineering and secure coding principles promoting cybersecurity-by-design and zero-trust architectures; establish security requirements for development environments; establish and implement security testing processes in the development life cycle; appropriately select, protect and manage security test data; and sanitise and anonymise testing data according to risk assessment.
Domain: SECURITY IN NETWORK AND INFORMATION SYSTEMS ACQUISITION, DEVELOPMENT AND MAINTENANCE
Implementation Guidance: A secure software development life cycle process should be implemented; smaller entities may use less demanding processes such as secure-by-design practices and security testing. Apply appropriate software testing methods including black-box, static and dynamic application security testing depending on requirement type. Test security at various SSDLC stages prior to go-live using independent tools and self-service platforms. When using production data or variations for testing, ensure proper sanitisation or anonymisation through masking, pseudonymization, deletion or redaction of identifiers, or one-way anonymisation. When using Free and Open Source Software, account for the voluntary nature of projects and assist dependent open source projects in adopting secure systems and coding principles where possible.

Control Description: The relevant entities shall take the appropriate measures to establish, document, implement and monitor configurations, including security configurations of hardware, software, services and networks.
Domain: SECURITY IN NETWORK AND INFORMATION SYSTEMS ACQUISITION, DEVELOPMENT AND MAINTENANCE
Implementation Guidance: Establish documented processes based on best practices and information security standards, maintaining detailed configuration settings for information processing, backup, scheduling, error handling, system recovery, cryptographic mechanisms, and audit trails. Document security-related parameters including registry settings, account and file permissions, and settings for functions, ports, protocols, services and remote connections. Employ automated mechanisms to centrally manage, apply and verify configuration settings for software, hardware, mobile devices and connected vehicles, implementing a configuration management database where appropriate to catalogue configuration items and their security attributes. Ensure all network, software and system configurations adhere to established security and operational standards, monitoring and controlling changes per policy. Identify and restrict unauthorized software through a deny-all, permit-by-exception policy for authorized software, managing separate secure baseline configurations for development/test and operational environments. Identify, document and approve deviations from established configuration settings based on defined operational exceptions.

Control Description: For the purpose of point 6.3.1., the relevant entities shall: (a) lay down and ensure security in configurations for their hardware, software, services and networks; (b) lay down and implement processes and tools to enforce the laid down secure configurations for hardware, software, services and networks, for newly installed systems as well as for systems in operation over their lifetime.
Domain: SECURITY IN NETWORK AND INFORMATION SYSTEMS ACQUISITION, DEVELOPMENT AND MAINTENANCE
Implementation Guidance: Base defined security configurations on hardening guides, least functionality and least privilege principles, testing before implementation. Establish, document and maintain configuration settings respecting the access control policy, employing security safeguards to detect and respond to unauthorized changes. Establish a configuration management plan containing roles, responsibilities, configuration management processes and procedures, a process for identifying configuration items throughout the system development life cycle, and a process for managing configuration items throughout their life cycle, protecting the plan from unauthorized disclosure and modification. For products lacking official updates after end of supplier support, implement enhanced controls including regular vulnerability scanning, strict configuration hardening, isolation where feasible and continuous monitoring.

Control Description: The relevant entities shall review and, where appropriate, update configurations at planned intervals or when significant incidents or significant changes to operations or risks occur.
Domain: SECURITY IN NETWORK AND INFORMATION SYSTEMS ACQUISITION, DEVELOPMENT AND MAINTENANCE
Implementation Guidance: Review and, where appropriate update (53) configurations at least monthly to ensure that patches have been applied, that the backup has been executed according to the plan and that monitoring is in place to identify and alert to fatal server/device/disk errors without delay. Regularly produce, keep and review change logs regarding the security configuration of information systems. Review and update the configurations after major changes (e.g. software updates) and past incidents. Where feasible, obtain baseline configuration files for key systems and devices to compare against current configurations.

Control Description: The relevant entities shall apply change management procedures to control changes of network and information systems. Where applicable, the procedures shall be consistent with the relevant entities’ general policies concerning change management.
Domain: SECURITY IN NETWORK AND INFORMATION SYSTEMS ACQUISITION, DEVELOPMENT AND MAINTENANCE
Implementation Guidance: Develop change management procedures based on industry-recognized and national standards, including request for change, risk assessment, categorization and prioritization criteria with associated testing and approval requirements, rollback requirements, and documentation and approval of changes. Allow different workflows based on system criticality, change scope and urgency, recording procedure steps followed for each change. Review and approve changes prior to implementation, testing procedures to ensure changes occur in a predefined way, and establishing a change advisory board where appropriate to evaluate requests based on risk, impact, resource requirements and business alignment. Conduct mandatory integrity checks before installing and deploying new software in an authenticated, authorised and non-repudiating manner. Test and validate changes before operational implementation, performing security impact analysis in separate test environments where appropriate, and taking necessary precautions such as backing up system images. Schedule, perform, document and review maintenance and repair records per supplier specifications and entity requirements, restricting maintenance tool use to authorized personnel and ensuring changes use only approved tools with documented execution.

Control Description: shall be applied for releases, modifications and emergency changes of any software and hardware in operation and changes to the configuration. The procedures shall ensure that those changes are documented and, based on the risk assessment carried out pursuant to point 2.1, tested and assessed in view of the potential impact before being implemented.
Domain: SECURITY IN NETWORK AND INFORMATION SYSTEMS ACQUISITION, DEVELOPMENT AND MAINTENANCE
Implementation Guidance: Consider a mandatory integrity check before installing and deploying new software. Ensure, where appropriate, that changes are done in an authenticated, authorised and non-repudiating manner. Test and validate changes before they are implemented in operational systems, where applicable. Where appropriate, a security impact analysis may be performed in a separate test environment before implementation in an operational environment. Take all necessary precautions before making changes (back up images, for instance). Schedule, perform, document and review records of maintenance and repairs on system components in accordance with the supplier’s specifications and/or the entity’s requirements. Ensure that changes are only allowed with approved tools and that their execution is documented. Restrict the use of maintenance tools to authorised personnel only.

Control Description: In the event that the regular change management procedures could not be followed due to an emergency, the relevant entities shall document the result of the change and the explanation for why the procedures could not be followed.
Domain: SECURITY IN NETWORK AND INFORMATION SYSTEMS ACQUISITION, DEVELOPMENT AND MAINTENANCE
Implementation Guidance: Where appropriate, integrate the pullback scenario (55) into the change management procedures. Assess the risks from legacy systems and upgrade existing legacy systems to include security mitigating measures in case appropriate security cannot be achieved. Make sure that regular change control procedures that could not be followed due to an emergency change are applied immediately after the emergency change.

Control Description: The relevant entities shall review and, where appropriate, update the procedures at planned intervals and when significant incidents or significant changes to operations or risks.
Domain: SECURITY IN NETWORK AND INFORMATION SYSTEMS ACQUISITION, DEVELOPMENT AND MAINTENANCE
Implementation Guidance: Review the change management procedures at least once every two years. Make sure that the management procedures cover planned and unplanned changes and the development phase, when applicable. Ensure that the process is not bypassed.

Control Description: The relevant entities shall establish, implement and apply a policy and procedures for security testing.
Domain: SECURITY IN NETWORK AND INFORMATION SYSTEMS ACQUISITION, DEVELOPMENT AND MAINTENANCE
Implementation Guidance: Take into account industry-recognised standards when developing the testing policy (56). Establish and maintain a testing programme appropriate to the entity’s size, complexity and maturity (57).
